one_line_takeaway: Invisible white-font PDF text injections using rubric-mimicking jailbreak prompts can flip LLM-as-judge peer-review decisions from Reject to Accept with up to 86.26% acceptance-rate increases in open-source models, while flagship proprietary models (GPT-5) resist the same attacks almost entirely but their distilled variant (GPT-5-Mini) and reasoning-heavy Gemini 2.5 Pro remain measurably susceptible.
key_finding: Open-source models are highly vulnerable and largely scale-independent in that vulnerability: mistral-small:22b showed an 86.26% increase in acceptance rate under the Disguise-and-Reconstruction strategy (Cls1DRA) and an 85.10% increase under Maximum Mark Magyk (Cls1MSM), with average score increases up to +13.95 (Cls1MSM on mistral-small) and +13.87/+13.53 (Cls1DRA on mistral-small/gemma3 respectively). tulu3:8b exhibited a 'Static Failure Mode,' inflating scores by a near-constant ~7.21 points across many unrelated strategies (e.g., +7.21 to +7.29 across Cls1SMCR through Cls3AE), suggesting a collapse into a default acceptance bucket rather than genuine attack processing. qwen3:30b was comparatively robust (score changes mostly between -0.82 and +2.254; several strategies produced 0.00% acceptance-rate change). Proprietary flagship models were far more robust than their distilled counterparts -- GPT-5 was near-flat across all 15 strategies (0.00% acceptance-rate increase for every strategy, max score shift -0.18 to +0.36) while GPT-5-Mini showed clear vulnerability clusters (e.g., +1.84 on Logic Decipherer, +1.60 on Evidence-Based Persuasion), which the authors term a 'safety tax' of distillation. Gemini-2.5-Pro showed the single highest closed-source vulnerability spike (+2.54 on Symbolic Masking & Context Redirection, 13.04% acceptance increase), which the authors attribute to a 'Reasoning Trap' where strong instruction-following makes camouflaged logic-puzzle-style attacks more effective. Class III (social-engineering / authority-based) strategies frequently backfired, producing negative score changes -- e.g., falcon3:10b penalized Social Proof by -4.07, gemma3:27b penalized Expert Endorsement by -3.95, and among proprietary models Social Proof triggered negative shifts for GPT-5 (-0.96), GPT-5-Mini (-0.89), and Claude Haiku 4.5 (-0.66). Token-level obfuscation attacks (Cls1MSM) that devastated open-source models were neutralized by proprietary models (GPT-5: -0.18, Claude Haiku 4.5: -0.16), indicating the vulnerability frontier for proprietary systems shifts from syntactic manipulation toward semantic/context-reframing attacks.
core_contribution: The paper presents the first systematic benchmark of indirect prompt-injection attacks against 'LLM-as-a-Judge' automated scientific peer review systems. The authors build a Domain-Specific Adversarial Adaptation Protocol (DSAAP) that adapts 15 canonical jailbreak strategies (grouped into three classes: Cognitive Obfuscation/Symbolic Masking, Teleological Deception/Context Reframing, and Epistemic Fabrication/Social Engineering) into 'Rubric-Isomorphic Payloads' designed to mimic the structure of the expected JSON review output. Attacks are injected as invisible text (white font, 1pt, bottom-right corner of the last page) into a curated 200-paper dataset (30 official conference templates with zero scientific content, 125 rejected ICLR 2025 OpenReview submissions, 30 accepted posters, 15 spotlights). A five-stage automated pipeline (Attack Injection -> MinerU PDF-to-Markdown ingestion -> System Prompt Definition with strict JSON schema and a Reviewer Bias Correction Layer -> Multi-Provider Inference across 13 models -> Analysis/Parsing) runs every (Model, Paper, Strategy) triplet and scores each on a 7-criterion, 0-5-per-criterion (max 35) rubric mapped to decision buckets from Strong Reject to Strong Accept. The paper also proposes a novel metric, the Weighted Adversarial Vulnerability Score (WAVS), which combines score-inflation magnitude, a step-function severity term for crossing the Reject/Accept semantic boundary, and alignment with ground-truth human decisions, explicitly to avoid treating all score inflation as equally severe.
threat_model: Adversary = a malicious paper author who wants an automated or human-assisted LLM reviewer to flip a 'Reject' decision to 'Accept'. Gray-box: the adversary has no access to model weights or the exact system prompt but understands the general LLM-as-a-Judge pipeline. The adversary can apply a perturbation delta to the submission PDF (xadv = x + delta) subject to three constraints: (C1) Format Compliance -- xadv must remain a valid, parseable PDF; (C2) Visual Imperceptibility -- the perturbation must be invisible to human reviewers (implemented as 1pt white-font text in a page margin); (C3) Output Validity -- the attack only counts if the model still returns syntactically valid JSON (refusals/malformed output are scored as failures, Sadv = Sorig). The adversary's hierarchical goal is (1) primary: force a categorical Reject->Accept decision flip, (2) secondary: maximize the scalar score. This is explicitly framed as an indirect prompt injection scenario (building on Greshake et al. 2023) situated in the 'Lazy Reviewer' setting where a human operator does not manually inspect the parsed text -- the authors state this is a worst-case assumption that does not model human-in-the-loop detection of visual anomalies.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations: (1) the 200-paper dataset is drawn primarily from Computer Science venues (ICLR, ACL), so findings on attack transferability may not generalize to disciplines with different reviewing norms (humanities, clinical sciences); (2) the threat model assumes a fully 'Lazy Reviewer' with no human-in-the-loop inspection of parsed text, which is an explicit worst-case posture and does not capture manual detection of visual anomalies like white-font injections; (3) proprietary model results are a time-bound snapshot subject to unannounced provider-side patches/RLHF updates, so vulnerability profiles may shift; (4) the study covers only textual/layout-based PDF injections and does not evaluate multi-modal adversarial attacks embedded in figures or charts.

datasets_used: Custom 200-paper dataset: 30 official conference templates (IEEE, ACL ARR, zero scientific content), 125 rejected real-world submissions and 30 accepted posters + 15 spotlights sourced from ICLR 2025 OpenReview
50-paper subset (15 template, 25 rejected, 5 poster, 5 spotlight) used specifically for closed-source/proprietary model evaluation
quantitative_results: mistral-small:22b: +86.26% acceptance-rate increase under Cls1DRA (Disguise and Reconstruction), +85.10% under Cls1MSM (Maximum Mark Magyk)
gemma3:27b average score increase: +13.53 (Cls1DRA), +12.59 (Cls1MSM); gemma3:27b acceptance-rate increase +80.60% (Cls1DRA), +81.64% (Cls2LDA)

tulu3:8b 'Static Failure Mode': ~+7.21 average score increase repeated near-identically across at least 6 strategies (Cls1SMCR, Cls2CRA, Cls2FA, Cls2LDA, Cls3AE, Cls3EBP, Cls3EE, Cls3LA, Cls3NEE, Cls3SP)
qwen3:30b: score changes largely bounded between -0.82 and +2.254; several strategies (Cls1SMCR, Cls2FA, Cls2SN, Cls2TF, Cls3AE, Cls3EE, Cls3NEE, Cls3SP) showed 0.00% acceptance-rate change
GPT-5: 0.00% acceptance-rate increase across all 15 strategies; score shifts ranged roughly -0.18 to +0.36
GPT-5-Mini: +1.84 (Cls2LDA), +1.60 (Cls3EBP) average score increase vs. GPT-5's +0.34 and -0.10 on the same strategies
Gemini-2.5-Pro: +2.54 average score increase and 13.04% acceptance-rate increase on Cls1SMCR (highest closed-source vulnerability spike); +1.96 / 8.70% on Cls2LDA
Cls1MSM (Maximum Mark Magyk) on proprietary models: GPT-5 -0.18, Claude Haiku 4.5 -0.16 (attack neutralized/backfired)
Cls3SP (Social Proof) backfire: falcon3:10b -4.07, gemma3:27b's Cls3EE -3.95, GPT-5 -0.96, GPT-5-Mini -0.89, Claude Haiku 4.5 -0.66
Relative Model Vulnerability Rate (RMVR): open-source falcon3 =100.0 (most vulnerable) down to qwen3=0.0 (least); closed-source gemini-2.5-flash=100.0 down to gpt-5=0.0
Comparative Strategy Effectiveness (CSE): Cls1MSM reached 100.0 on open-source models and 100.0 (Cls2LDA) on closed-source models as most effective strategies; Cls3AE reached 0.0 on both
baselines_compared: Un-injected/original paper score (per-model, per-paper baseline) used to compute score inflation for every attack strategy

13 models compared against each other: open-source (tulu3-8B, llama3.1-8B, falcon3-10B, gpt-oss-20B, mistral-small-22B, gemma3-27B, qwen3-30B, deepseek-r1-32B) vs proprietary (Claude Haiku 4.5, Gemini 2.5 Flash, Gemini 2.5 Pro, GPT-5-Mini, GPT-5)
15 jailbreak strategies compared against each other via WAVS-derived Comparative Strategy Effectiveness (CSE)
